lessons.title: The Big Idea: Signals Are Made of Simple Waves
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Press several piano keys at once and you hear a single, complex sound. Yet a trained musician can name the individual notes inside that chord. A *transform* is the math that does the same trick for any signal: it reads the simple notes hiding inside a complicated wave.

**Build.** A **signal** is just something that changes over time, like the air pressure of a sound or the voltage in a wire. The simplest signal is a single smooth wave (a **sine wave**), which is like one pure musical note. The huge, surprising idea behind this whole topic is this: *almost any signal can be built by adding together simple sine waves of different speeds.* A fast wave wiggles many times per second (high pitch); a slow wave wiggles few times per second (low pitch). How fast a wave wiggles is its **frequency**, measured in **hertz (Hz)**, meaning wiggles per second.

We usually picture a signal in the **time domain**: the horizontal axis is time, and we watch the value go up and down. A **transform** rewrites that same signal in the **frequency domain**: the horizontal axis is now frequency, and the height at each spot tells you *how much* of that frequency is present. Going to the frequency domain is like reading the sheet music for a sound: instead of the wiggly waveform, you see a list of the notes inside it.

When a signal repeats over and over (a **periodic** signal, like a steady musical tone), the recipe of waves inside it is called the **Fourier series**. Its frequency-domain picture is a set of separate spikes, called a **spectrum**: one spike for each pure wave present, placed at that wave's frequency, as tall as that wave is strong.

**Recap.** A transform turns a wiggly time signal into a spectrum that shows which frequencies (which pure waves) it is made of. This single idea powers audio, images, and communications. The questions below build that intuition.

items.prompt: In everyday terms, what does a transform do to a signal?
choices: It makes the signal louder
It rewrites the signal as a list of the simple waves (frequencies) it is made of
It deletes the high-frequency parts of the signal
It speeds the signal up so it plays faster
items.explanation: A transform does not change how loud or fast a signal is; it re-describes the same signal in a new way: which pure waves are inside it. The musical-chord analogy is exact: the chord still sounds the same, but now you can name its notes.

items.prompt: A signal is shown with time on the horizontal axis. This view is called the:
choices: Frequency domain
Time domain
Spectrum
Phase domain
items.explanation: When the horizontal axis is time and you watch the value rise and fall, you are looking at the time domain. The frequency domain is the other view, where the horizontal axis is frequency instead of time.

items.prompt: What does 'frequency' measure, and what unit is it in?
choices: How tall a wave is, measured in volts
How many times a wave wiggles per second, measured in hertz (Hz)
How long a signal lasts, measured in seconds
How loud a sound is, measured in decibels
items.explanation: Frequency is the wiggle rate: cycles per second, called hertz (Hz). A high frequency is a fast wiggle (high pitch); a low frequency is a slow wiggle (low pitch). Height is amplitude, not frequency.

items.prompt: The 'big idea' that makes all of this possible is that almost any signal can be built by:
choices: Adding together simple sine waves of different frequencies
Multiplying two random numbers together
Drawing a single straight line
Recording it at a higher volume
items.explanation: The central insight is that complicated signals are just sums of simple sine waves. That is why finding 'which sine waves, and how much of each' (the spectrum) fully describes the signal.

items.prompt: A spectrum of a repeating musical tone shows several separate spikes. What does the HEIGHT of a spike tell you?
choices: How long the note lasts
How strong (how much) that particular frequency is in the signal
The exact time the note was played
Whether the signal is digital or analog
items.explanation: In a spectrum, position (left-right) is frequency and height is amount. A tall spike means that frequency is a strong ingredient; a short spike means it is a faint ingredient. Timing information is not shown in this simple spectrum view.

items.prompt: The musical-chord analogy compares the Fourier transform to:
choices: Turning up the volume on a speaker
Reading the sheet music (the individual notes) from a sound
Erasing a recording
Plugging in a microphone
items.explanation: A chord is several notes played together; the transform reveals the separate notes, just like sheet music lists them. That is exactly what going to the frequency domain does for any signal.

items.prompt: When a signal repeats over and over (a periodic signal), the recipe of simple waves inside it is called the:
choices: Laplace transform
Fourier series
z-transform
Time constant
items.explanation: For a repeating signal, the list of its ingredient waves is the Fourier series. (The Laplace and z-transforms, covered later, are related tools for other situations.)

items.prompt: If you combine a slow (low-frequency) wave and a fast (high-frequency) wave, the result is:
choices: Still a single simple sine wave
Silence, because they cancel out
A more complicated wave that contains both frequencies
Only the faster wave; the slow one disappears
items.explanation: Adding waves together produces a more complex waveform that holds both frequencies. The transform's job is to look at that combined wave and recover the two original frequencies, exactly as the diagram shows.

items.prompt: Which of these is the best one-sentence summary of the time domain versus the frequency domain?
choices: The time domain shows the signal's color; the frequency domain shows its weight
The time domain shows how the signal changes moment to moment; the frequency domain shows which frequencies it is made of
They are two completely unrelated signals
The frequency domain always has more energy than the time domain
items.explanation: Both views describe the SAME signal. Time domain: value versus time. Frequency domain: amount versus frequency. A transform is just the recipe for switching between these two descriptions.

lessons.title: The Fourier Transform: A Spectrum for Any Signal
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A single hand-clap does not repeat, so it has no steady set of notes. Yet it still has a 'sound color': mostly a quick burst of many frequencies. How do we find the frequency recipe of a signal that happens only once? That is the job of the **Fourier transform**.

**Build.** The Fourier series (last lesson) works for signals that repeat forever and gives a spectrum of separate spikes. Most real signals, like a clap or a spoken word, do not repeat. The **Fourier transform** handles these one-time (**aperiodic**) signals. Its spectrum is no longer separate spikes but a smooth curve: every frequency can be present in some amount. We write the spectrum as $X(f)$, meaning 'the amount of frequency $f$ in the signal.' In words: the Fourier transform takes a signal in time and produces its full frequency recipe as a continuous curve.

A few easy-to-picture rules follow. A very short, sharp signal (a quick spike) contains *many* frequencies, so its spectrum is wide. A long, slow signal contains *few* frequencies, so its spectrum is narrow. This is the **time-bandwidth trade**: short in time means wide in frequency, and vice versa. Delaying a signal (playing it later) does not change *which* frequencies are present, only their timing, so the spectrum's shape stays the same.

The most useful rule is about **convolution**. Convolution is a 'sliding overlap' operation: it is what happens when a signal passes through a system like a filter or an echo-causing room. Doing convolution directly is slow and fiddly. The headline result: **convolution in the time domain equals simple multiplication in the frequency domain.** So instead of the messy sliding overlap, you can transform both signals to the frequency domain, multiply them point by point, and transform back. A device that processes signals this way (an amplifier, a filter, a room with echo) is called an **LTI system**, and its frequency-domain behaviour, called the **frequency response**, simply tells you how much each frequency is boosted or cut.

**Recap.** The Fourier transform gives a frequency recipe for *any* signal, even one that happens once, and its star rule, convolution becomes multiplication, is why filtering is so much easier in the frequency domain.

items.prompt: How is the Fourier transform different from the Fourier series?
choices: It only works on digital signals
It handles one-time (aperiodic) signals and gives a smooth spectrum, not separate spikes
It removes all the frequencies from a signal
It only works on signals that repeat forever
items.explanation: The Fourier series is for repeating signals and gives discrete spikes. The Fourier transform extends the idea to signals that happen only once, producing a continuous spectrum where any frequency can appear.

items.prompt: What does the notation $X(f)$ mean?
choices: The signal's value at time $f$
The amount of frequency $f$ that is present in the signal
The total length of the signal in seconds
The signal multiplied by itself
items.explanation: $X(f)$ is the spectrum: for each frequency $f$, it tells you how much of that frequency the signal contains. It is the frequency-domain description of the signal.

items.prompt: A very short, sharp spike in time has a spectrum that is:
choices: Very narrow (only one frequency)
Wide (it contains many frequencies)
Exactly zero everywhere
Always a single straight line
items.explanation: This is the time-bandwidth trade: a brief, sharp event in time needs many frequencies added together to make that sharpness, so its spectrum is wide. Long, slow signals are the opposite: narrow in frequency.

items.prompt: The 'time-bandwidth trade' says that a signal which is long and slow in time will be:
choices: Wide in frequency
Narrow in frequency
Silent
Negative in frequency
items.explanation: Short in time means wide in frequency; long in time means narrow in frequency. A slow, drawn-out tone is close to a single pure frequency, so its spectrum is narrow.

items.prompt: What is 'convolution' in plain terms?
choices: Adding two numbers together
A sliding-overlap operation that describes a signal passing through a system like a filter or an echoey room
Deleting the loudest frequency
Reversing a signal in time only
items.explanation: Convolution is the math of one signal being reshaped by a system: a filter, an echo, a blur. It combines the input with the system's response by sliding one across the other and summing the overlap.

items.prompt: The headline rule of the Fourier transform is that convolution in time becomes what in frequency?
choices: Convolution again
Addition
Simple point-by-point multiplication
Subtraction
items.explanation: Convolution in time equals multiplication in frequency. This is why filtering is easy in the frequency domain: instead of a messy sliding overlap, you just multiply the two spectra together.

items.prompt: Why is 'convolution becomes multiplication' so useful for filtering a signal?
choices: It makes the signal louder automatically
Multiplying spectra point by point is far simpler than doing the sliding-overlap convolution directly
It removes the need to know anything about the filter
It deletes all noise perfectly
items.explanation: Direct convolution is slow and fiddly. The rule lets you transform to the frequency domain, multiply (easy!), and transform back. This swap from convolution to multiplication is the practical heart of digital filtering.

items.prompt: An 'LTI system' (like an amplifier or filter) is fully described in the frequency domain by its:
choices: Color
Frequency response, which says how much each frequency is boosted or cut
Total weight
Serial number
items.explanation: The frequency response is the system's fingerprint: for every frequency it tells you the gain (boost or cut) applied. Filtering then is just multiplying the input spectrum by this frequency response.

items.prompt: If you delay a signal so it plays a little later, what happens to which frequencies are present?
choices: All the high frequencies disappear
Nothing; the same frequencies are present, just timed differently
New frequencies are created
The signal becomes silent
items.explanation: A pure delay shifts the signal in time but does not change its frequency content. The amount of each frequency (the spectrum's shape) is unchanged; only the timing shifts.

items.prompt: A microphone records a single hand-clap, which does not repeat. Can the Fourier transform find its frequency content?
choices: No, because only repeating signals have a spectrum
Yes; the Fourier transform works on one-time signals and gives a smooth spectrum of the frequencies in the clap
No, because a clap has no frequencies at all
Only if the clap is exactly one second long
items.explanation: This is exactly what the Fourier transform is for. A clap is aperiodic, so it has a smooth (continuous) spectrum rather than separate spikes, but it absolutely has a frequency recipe the transform can reveal.

lessons.title: The Laplace Transform: Will It Settle Down or Blow Up?
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Push a child on a swing and let go: the swinging slowly dies down. Tap a microphone too close to its speaker and you get a screech that grows louder and louder. The difference between 'settles down' and 'blows up' is called **stability**, and the **Laplace transform** is the tool engineers use to predict it before building anything.

**Build.** The Laplace transform is a close cousin of the Fourier transform with one extra power: besides asking 'which frequencies are present?' it also asks 'do they grow, hold steady, or fade away over time?' To capture both ideas at once, it uses a special variable written $s$. You can think of $s$ as carrying two pieces of information: how fast something wiggles (frequency) and whether it is fading or growing (decay or growth).

The payoff is the **transfer function**, written $H(s)$. A transfer function is a compact description of how a system (a circuit, a control loop, a filter) responds. It is written as one expression divided by another, and the special values of $s$ that make it behave dramatically have names. The values that make the bottom blow up to infinity are called **poles**; the values that make the top drop to zero are called **zeros**. Poles are the stars of the show: each pole describes one natural behaviour of the system, like the swing's gentle fade or the microphone's runaway screech.

Here is the beautiful, simple rule. Plot the poles on a flat map where left-to-right means decay-or-growth and up-down means frequency. If *every* pole sits on the **left** side, the system is **stable**: every natural motion fades away, like the swing. If even one pole sits on the **right** side, the system is **unstable**: something grows without bound, like the feedback screech. A pole right on the dividing line keeps wiggling forever without fading. So checking stability becomes as easy as asking: are all the poles on the left?

**Recap.** The Laplace transform tracks both frequency and fading using the variable $s$, summarises a system as a transfer function with poles and zeros, and tells you at a glance whether the system will settle down (all poles on the left) or blow up (any pole on the right).

items.prompt: What extra question does the Laplace transform answer that the basic Fourier transform does not?
choices: What color the signal is
Whether the frequencies in a system grow, hold steady, or fade away over time
How much the signal costs to record
The exact spelling of the signal's name
items.explanation: Fourier asks which frequencies are present. Laplace adds the crucial 'and do they grow or decay?' This is exactly what you need to judge stability, the difference between a fading swing and a growing screech.

items.prompt: In the Laplace transform, the variable $s$ carries information about:
choices: Only the loudness of the signal
Both how fast something wiggles (frequency) and whether it fades or grows
Only the color of the signal
The price of the equipment
items.explanation: The variable $s$ bundles two ideas: a frequency part (the wiggle rate) and a decay-or-growth part. That second part is what lets Laplace reason about stability.

items.prompt: A 'transfer function' $H(s)$ describes:
choices: How much a signal weighs
How a system (circuit, filter, control loop) responds, written as one expression over another
The temperature of the room
A random list of numbers
items.explanation: A transfer function is a compact recipe for a system's behaviour. Because it is a ratio (top over bottom), its special values (poles and zeros) reveal the system's natural behaviours.

items.prompt: In a transfer function, what is a 'pole'?
choices: A value of $s$ that makes the function drop to zero
A value of $s$ that makes the function blow up to infinity, describing a natural behaviour of the system
The loudest frequency in the signal
The name of the horizontal axis
items.explanation: Poles come from the bottom of the transfer function blowing up. Each pole corresponds to one natural motion of the system. Zeros, by contrast, are where the top makes the function drop to zero.

items.prompt: On the s-plane map, what does it mean if ALL of a system's poles sit on the LEFT side?
choices: The system is unstable and blows up
The system is stable; every natural motion fades away over time
The system has no frequencies
The system is digital
items.explanation: Left side means fading. If every pole is on the left, every natural behaviour dies down, like the swing slowing to a stop. That is the definition of a stable system.

items.prompt: A microphone-and-speaker feedback screech that grows louder and louder corresponds to a pole that is:
choices: On the left side (stable)
On the right side (unstable, grows without bound)
Exactly at the center
Off the map entirely
items.explanation: A growing, runaway response means a pole on the right side of the s-plane. Right side equals growth; any pole there makes the whole system unstable, no matter where the others are.

items.prompt: A system has poles at the left side AND one pole on the right side. Overall, the system is:
choices: Stable, because most poles are on the left
Unstable, because even one right-side pole makes something grow without bound
Stable, because the left poles cancel the right one
Impossible to have
items.explanation: Stability requires EVERY pole to be on the left. A single right-side pole contributes a growing behaviour that eventually dominates, so the system is unstable regardless of the others.

items.prompt: What does a pole sitting exactly on the dividing line (the vertical axis) mean?
choices: The behaviour fades away quickly
The behaviour keeps wiggling forever without fading or growing
The system has no poles
The signal is silent
items.explanation: On the dividing line there is no fading and no growth, so that natural motion oscillates forever at a steady size. This is the borderline case between stable (left) and unstable (right).

items.prompt: On the s-plane, the up-down (vertical) direction represents:
choices: How fast something wiggles (its frequency)
The price of the system
The number of poles
The color of the signal
items.explanation: Up-down is frequency (how fast it oscillates), while left-right is decay-or-growth. A pole far up oscillates fast; a pole far left fades fast.

items.prompt: Why do engineers like checking stability with the Laplace transform before building a system?
choices: It makes the system cheaper to buy
Just by looking at where the poles sit, they can predict whether the design will settle down or blow up
It removes the need for any wiring
It guarantees the system will be silent
items.explanation: The pole locations give an at-a-glance stability check: all poles on the left means safe, any on the right means trouble. That lets problems be caught on paper before anything is built.

lessons.title: Digital Spectra: the DFT, the Fast FFT, and Leakage
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Open a music app and watch the dancing bars that jump with the beat. Each bar is a frequency, and its height shows how much of that frequency is playing right now. Those bars are computed by a method called the **FFT**, the workhorse that lets computers find the spectrum of a signal thousands of times a second.

**Build.** Computers cannot handle a smooth, never-ending curve; they work with **samples**, a list of measurements taken at a steady rate. The rate of measuring is the **sample rate**, written $f_s$ (samples per second). Suppose you take $N$ samples. The tool that finds the spectrum of those $N$ samples is the **DFT** (Discrete Fourier Transform). It produces $N$ numbers, and each is called a **bin**, a little bucket that collects one specific frequency. Picture the dancing bars: each bar is a bin.

The bins are evenly spaced in frequency. The spacing between neighbouring bins is $$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N}.$$ In words: bin spacing equals the sample rate divided by the number of samples. The practical takeaway: to tell two close-together frequencies apart, you need *more samples* (a longer recording), which makes the bins finer. A faster sample rate alone does not help with that.

The **FFT** (Fast Fourier Transform) is not a different result; it is a clever shortcut that computes the exact same DFT bins but enormously faster, by reusing repeated work. 'Fast' is the whole point: it is what makes real-time spectrum bars, voice apps, and image tools possible.

Two real-world gotchas. **Leakage:** if a tone's frequency does not land exactly on a bin's center, its energy smears into the neighbouring bins, blurring the picture, the way light scatters around a too-bright spot. The fix is **windowing**: gently fading the recording in and out at its ends before the FFT. A **window** (with names like Hann or Hamming) greatly reduces the smearing, at the small cost of making each peak a little wider.

**Recap.** The DFT turns $N$ samples into $N$ frequency bins spaced $f_s/N$ apart; the FFT computes them fast; finer resolution needs more samples; and windowing tames the leakage that appears when a tone falls between bins.

items.prompt: The dancing bars in a music visualizer each represent one:
choices: Second of the song
Frequency bin, whose height shows how much of that frequency is playing
Speaker in the room
Letter of the song's title
items.explanation: Each bar is a frequency bin from the FFT. Its position is a frequency and its height is how much of that frequency is present right now, exactly the spectrum idea applied in real time.

items.prompt: What are 'samples' of a signal?
choices: Free giveaways at a store
Measurements of the signal taken at a steady rate, turning it into a list of numbers a computer can use
The loudest moments of the signal
The colors inside the signal
items.explanation: A computer cannot store a smooth continuous curve, so it records the signal's value at regular instants. Those measurements are samples, and how often you take them is the sample rate.

items.prompt: The DFT takes $N$ samples and produces:
choices: A single number
$N$ frequency bins, each holding the amount of one specific frequency
A smooth never-ending curve
The original signal, unchanged
items.explanation: The DFT (Discrete Fourier Transform) converts $N$ time samples into $N$ frequency bins. Each bin is a bucket for one frequency, and together they form the digital spectrum.

items.prompt: You sample at $f_s = 8000$ Hz and take $N = 1000$ samples. What is the spacing between frequency bins?
choices: 8 Hz
items.explanation: Bin spacing is $\\Delta f = f_s / N = 8000 / 1000 = 8$ Hz. Each bin sits 8 Hz apart from its neighbours.

items.prompt: To tell two very close frequencies apart, what do you most need?
choices: A louder signal
More samples (a longer recording), which makes the bins finer
A different colored cable
Fewer samples
items.explanation: Finer resolution comes from a longer recording (more samples), which shrinks the bin spacing $f_s/N$. Just turning up the volume or shortening the recording will not separate close frequencies.

items.prompt: How is the FFT related to the DFT?
choices: The FFT gives totally different answers from the DFT
The FFT is a fast shortcut that computes the exact same DFT bins, just much quicker
The FFT only works on sound, not images
The FFT is slower than the DFT
items.explanation: Same numbers, faster method. The FFT reuses repeated calculations to get the identical DFT result far more quickly, which is why it is used everywhere in real-time processing.

items.prompt: Why does the FFT being 'fast' matter so much?
choices: It makes the answer more accurate than a slow method
Speed is what makes real-time uses possible, like live spectrum bars, voice apps, and image tools
It uses less electricity than a battery
It changes which frequencies are present
items.explanation: The FFT does not change the result, only the time it takes. That speed is the enabler: without it, computing spectra thousands of times per second would be impractical.

items.prompt: What is 'leakage' in a digital spectrum?
choices: A signal escaping from a broken wire
When a tone's frequency lands between bins, its energy smears into neighbouring bins and blurs the picture
Losing samples because the disk is full
A frequency becoming louder over time
items.explanation: If a tone does not sit exactly on a bin center, the FFT cannot place it in one clean bucket, so its energy spreads into nearby bins. That smearing is leakage, like light scattering around a too-bright spot.

items.prompt: What does applying a 'window' (like Hann or Hamming) do before an FFT?
choices: It deletes the signal entirely
It gently fades the recording in and out at the ends, greatly reducing leakage
It increases the sample rate
It makes the signal repeat forever
items.explanation: A window tapers the recording's ends toward zero so the FFT does not see an abrupt cut-off. This sharply reduces leakage, at the small cost of making each peak slightly wider.

items.prompt: What is the trade-off when you apply a window to reduce leakage?
choices: The signal gets louder but noisier
Each frequency peak becomes a little wider
All frequencies disappear
There is no trade-off at all
items.explanation: Windowing cleans up the smearing (good) but in exchange each peak broadens a bit, slightly blurring very close frequencies. You trade a little sharpness for a much cleaner picture overall.

items.prompt: A friend says 'I will just record for a shorter time but at a higher sample rate to separate two close tones.' Why is this wrong?
choices: Higher sample rates are illegal
Telling close frequencies apart needs more samples (longer recording); a shorter record gives coarser bins no matter the sample rate
A higher sample rate erases the signal
Close tones can never be separated by any method
items.explanation: Resolution is set by record length through $\\Delta f = f_s/N$. A short recording means small $N$ and coarse bins. To separate close tones you need a longer recording, i.e. more genuine samples.

lessons.title: The z-Transform, Digital Filters, and How It All Fits Together
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Your phone's voice app cleans up background hiss, and a music app can boost the bass. Both rely on **digital filters**: small programs that reshape a stream of samples. The tool for designing and checking these filters is the **z-transform**, the digital-world cousin of the Laplace transform.

**Build.** Recall that the Laplace transform helped us judge whether a continuous system would settle down or blow up by looking at where its poles sat. The **z-transform** does the very same job for **digital** systems, the ones that work on samples instead of a smooth signal. It uses a variable called $z$, and its key building block is a **one-sample delay**, written $z^{-1}$, meaning 'use the value from one step ago.' Digital filters are built almost entirely from delays, multiplies, and adds, so $z$ is the natural language for them.

Just like Laplace, a digital filter has a transfer function with **poles** and **zeros**. But the stability map is now a **circle** instead of a left/right split. Picture a flat map with a circle of radius one (the **unit circle**) drawn on it. The simple rule: a digital filter is **stable** if *all* its poles sit **inside** the unit circle. A pole outside the circle means a runaway, growing output (unstable); a pole right on the circle keeps ringing forever. Poles placed just inside the circle create strong, resonant peaks, useful for emphasising a frequency; zeros placed on the circle create deep notches that cut a frequency out.

**The big picture.** All of these transforms are one family answering one question, 'what frequencies are inside this signal?', for different situations. Fourier handles ordinary continuous signals; Laplace adds the grow-or-fade question for continuous systems (poles in a left/right map); the FFT/DFT handle sampled signals on a computer; and the z-transform adds the grow-or-fade question for digital systems (poles in an inside/outside circle map). The recurring theme: **wherever a signal repeats or is sampled, its frequency picture becomes a set of discrete points**, and stability is always about where the poles sit relative to a boundary.

**Recap.** The z-transform is Laplace for digital systems: it uses delays ($z^{-1}$), describes filters with poles and zeros, and judges stability by whether all poles lie inside the unit circle, completing a family of transforms that all reveal a signal's hidden frequencies.

items.prompt: The z-transform is best described as:
choices: A way to make signals louder
The digital-world cousin of the Laplace transform, used to design and check digital filters
A tool only for continuous (non-sampled) signals
A method for storing pictures
items.explanation: Laplace handles continuous systems; the z-transform does the same job for digital systems that work on samples. It is the natural tool for digital filters.

items.prompt: In the z-transform, what does the building block $z^{-1}$ represent?
choices: Multiplying the signal by negative one
A one-sample delay: use the value from one step ago
Deleting the signal
Doubling the sample rate
items.explanation: $z^{-1}$ means 'one sample ago.' Digital filters are built from delays like this, plus multiplies and adds, which is why $z$ is the natural language for them.

items.prompt: What is a 'digital filter'?
choices: A physical screen on a camera lens
A small program that reshapes a stream of samples, for example to cut hiss or boost bass
A type of battery
A way to store music on a disk
items.explanation: A digital filter processes a sequence of samples to change its frequency content, such as reducing background noise or emphasising the bass. The z-transform is how we design and analyse them.

items.prompt: For a digital filter, the boundary that decides stability is:
choices: A left-versus-right line
A circle of radius one, called the unit circle
A single point at the center
The top edge of the screen
items.explanation: In the digital (z) world, stability is judged against the unit circle, not a left/right line. This is the digital mirror of Laplace's left-half-plane rule.

items.prompt: A digital filter is stable when all of its poles are:
choices: Outside the unit circle
Inside the unit circle
Exactly on the unit circle
At the very center only
items.explanation: Inside the unit circle means the natural behaviours fade away, so the filter is stable. Poles outside grow without bound (unstable); poles right on the circle ring forever.

items.prompt: A digital filter has a pole sitting OUTSIDE the unit circle. The filter is:
choices: Stable
Unstable; its output grows without bound
Silent
Perfectly clean
items.explanation: A pole outside the unit circle produces a runaway, growing output, which is the definition of an unstable filter. For stability every pole must be strictly inside.

items.prompt: Placing a zero right on the unit circle at some frequency does what to the filter?
choices: Creates a strong resonant peak there
Creates a deep notch that cuts out that frequency
Makes the filter unstable
Has no effect at all
items.explanation: A zero on the unit circle forces the response down to nothing at that frequency, a notch. Poles do the opposite: poles near the circle create peaks. This is how filters are shaped.

items.prompt: A pole placed just INSIDE the unit circle (close to it) produces:
choices: A deep notch
A strong, resonant peak that emphasises that frequency
Total silence
An unstable, growing output
items.explanation: A pole near (but inside) the circle gives a sharp resonant boost at that frequency while still being stable. The closer to the circle, the sharper the peak.

items.prompt: Across all the transforms, what single question are they all answering?
choices: How heavy is the signal?
What frequencies are inside this signal?
What color is the signal?
How much does the equipment cost?
items.explanation: Fourier, Laplace, the DFT/FFT, and the z-transform are one family. They all reveal the frequency content of a signal; they differ in whether the signal is continuous or sampled, and whether they also track growth/decay for stability.

items.prompt: In the 'big picture', the z-transform plays the same role for digital systems that which transform plays for continuous systems?
choices: The Fourier series
The Laplace transform
The DFT
None of them
items.explanation: Laplace judges stability of continuous systems with poles on a left/right map; the z-transform does the same for digital systems with poles inside/outside the unit circle. They are direct counterparts.

items.prompt: A recurring theme tying the whole topic together is that wherever a signal repeats or is sampled, its frequency picture becomes:
choices: A smooth never-ending curve
A set of discrete points (separate spikes or bins)
Completely empty
Impossible to compute
items.explanation: Repetition (Fourier series) or sampling (DFT/FFT) turns the frequency picture into discrete points rather than a smooth curve. And in every case, stability comes down to where the poles sit relative to a boundary.
